questions.promptTex: \text{Empirical rule: about what \% of data falls within } 2\sigma \text{ of the mean? (no \% sign)}
questions.explanation: Empirical rule: about 95% within $\pm 2\sigma$.

questions.promptTex: \text{SAT scores: } \mu = 1000, \sigma = 200. \text{ Your score: } 1400. \text{ z-score?}
steps: You scored 2 standard deviations above the mean.

questions.promptTex: \text{Adult male heights are approximately } N(70, 3) \text{ inches.} \\ \text{About what \% are between 67 and 73 inches?}
questions.explanation: $67 = 70 - 3 = \mu - \sigma$, $73 = 70 + 3 = \mu + \sigma$. Within $\pm 1\sigma$ → 68%.

questions.promptTex: \text{Same heights } N(70, 3). \text{ About what \% are above 76 inches?}
hints: Symmetry: half on each side → 2.5% above 76.
questions.explanation: $76 = \mu + 2\sigma$. 5% outside $\pm 2\sigma$; by symmetry, 2.5% above.
steps: By symmetry, half are above $\mu + 2\sigma$ and half below $\mu - 2\sigma$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the standard normal distribution?}
questions.explanation: Standard normal: $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$, written $N(0, 1)$.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } X \sim N(50, 10), \text{ find the z-score of } x = 65.
\text{Test 1: } \mu = 75, \sigma = 10, \text{ score 88. Test 2: } \mu = 60, \sigma = 5, \text{ score 70.} \\ \text{Which is the better performance (relative to peers)?}
hints: Higher z = farther above mean = better.
questions.explanation: Test 1 z = 1.3, Test 2 z = 2.0. Test 2 is the better performance (2 σ above mean).
steps: Test 2 is **2 standard deviations** above its mean — better than Test 1's **1.3**.

questions.promptTex: \text{If you increase } \sigma \text{ in a normal distribution, the curve becomes:}
hints: Wider curve, but lower peak (total area still 1).
questions.explanation: Larger σ spreads the data more → wider, shorter curve. (Total area must remain 1, so wider means lower peak.)
steps: Larger σ → data is more spread out → curve is **wider**.
Total area under curve = 1 (probability), so wider means **lower peak** to compensate.

questions.promptTex: \text{For standard normal: } P(Z < 0) = ? \text{ (decimal)}
hints: Symmetry: half the curve is below 0.
questions.explanation: By symmetry of the bell curve around 0: half the area is below 0. $P(Z < 0) = 0.5$.
steps: Standard normal is symmetric around its mean (0).
Half the area is to the left of 0, half to the right.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } N(\mu, \sigma): \text{ about what fraction is between } \mu - 3\sigma \text{ and } \mu + 3\sigma?
questions.explanation: $\pm 3\sigma$ contains about 99.7% of data.

questions.promptTex: \text{IQ scores: } N(100, 15). \text{ A score of } 130 \text{ has } z = ?
questions.explanation: $z = (130 - 100)/15 = 2$. Two SDs above the mean (top ~2.5%).
steps: (IQ of 130 corresponds to about the 97.5th percentile.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Why is the area under a normal curve always 1?}
hints: All probabilities sum to 1 — that's a fundamental property.
By definition for any continuous distribution.
questions.explanation: The total area under any probability density curve = 1, by definition. (P(any outcome) = 1.)
steps: For continuous distributions, probability is the area under the curve.
So total area = 1 for any probability distribution.
(This is true for normal AND every other distribution.)

questions.promptTex: \text{For the standard normal, find } P(Z < 1). \\ \text{Use the table value } \Phi(1) = 0.8413.
questions.explanation: $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal. From the table: $\Phi(1) = 0.8413$ — i.e., about $84\%$ of standard-normal values are at most 1 SD above the mean.
steps: **Recognize the notation.** $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ is the standard-normal CDF — the area under the bell curve to the LEFT of $z$.
**Empirical-rule sanity check.** $68\%$ within $\pm 1\sigma$ → $34\%$ between $0$ and $+1$. Plus the $50\%$ below $0$ gives $84\%$ below $+1$. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{For the standard normal, find } P(Z > 1.5). \\ \text{Use } \Phi(1.5) = 0.9332. \text{ Round to 4 decimals.}
questions.explanation: Tails: $P(Z > z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. So $P(Z > 1.5) = 1 - 0.9332 = 0.0668$ — about $6.7\%$ of standard-normal values exceed $1.5$.
steps: **Z-table gives left-tail probabilities.** $\Phi(z) = P(Z \leq z)$ is the LEFT area. We want the RIGHT area beyond $z = 1.5$.
**Mental check.** $\Phi(1.5)$ is $93\%$ish, so $7\%$ish is in the right tail. Matches.

**Pattern lesson — three operations.** Combining $\Phi$ values: (a) left tail = $\Phi(z)$, (b) right tail = $1 - \Phi(z)$, (c) between $a$ and $b$ = $\Phi(b) - \Phi(a)$. Every z-table problem reduces to one of these.

questions.promptTex: \text{Standard normal: find } P(-1 < Z < 1). \\ \text{Use } \Phi(1) = 0.8413. \text{ Round to 4 decimals.}
questions.explanation: $P(-1 < Z < 1) = \Phi(1) - \Phi(-1)$. By symmetry $\Phi(-1) = 1 - \Phi(1) = 0.1587$. So $P = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826$. This is the $68\%$ of the empirical rule computed precisely.
steps: **Use symmetry for $\Phi(-1)$.** By the symmetry of the bell curve about 0: $\Phi(-z) = 1 - \Phi(z)$. So $\Phi(-1) = 1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$.

**Empirical-rule connection.** This is the famous $68\%$ — within $\pm 1\sigma$ of the mean. The empirical rule's '$68\%$' is the rounded $0.6826$. Two ways of getting the same fact: rule-of-thumb vs precise z-table.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } X \sim N(70, 5), \text{ find } P(X < 80). \\ \text{Use } \Phi(2) = 0.9772.
questions.explanation: Standardize first: $z = (80 - 70)/5 = 2$. Then look up: $P(X < 80) = \Phi(2) = 0.9772$. About $97.7\%$ of values fall below $80$ for this normal distribution.
steps: **Pattern lesson — the universal recipe.** For ANY normal probability: (1) standardize to a $z$-score, (2) look up $\Phi(z)$, (3) combine with $1$, complement, or other $\Phi$ values as needed. Same three steps for every problem.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } X \sim N(70, 5), \text{ find } P(60 < X < 80). \\ \text{Use } \Phi(2) = 0.9772. \text{ Round to 4 decimals.}
hints: (This is the empirical rule's '$95\%$' computed precisely.)
questions.explanation: Standardize both bounds: $z = -2$ to $z = 2$. By symmetry $\Phi(-2) = 1 - \Phi(2) = 0.0228$. $P(-2 < Z < 2) = 0.9772 - 0.0228 = 0.9544$. The empirical rule's '$95\%$' is really $95.44\%$.
steps: **Empirical-rule precision.** This is the $95\%$ of the empirical rule. The rule-of-thumb says $\pm 2\sigma$ ≈ $95\%$. Actual is $95.44\%$ — close enough for quick estimates, but use the table when precision matters.

questions.promptTex: \text{For SAT scores } X \sim N(1000, 200), \\ \text{find the 90th percentile (the score with } 90\% \text{ of scores below it). Use } \Phi(1.28) = 0.90.
hints: Find $z$ such that $\Phi(z) = 0.90$: from the table, $z \approx 1.28$.
questions.explanation: Reverse direction: from probability to $z$, then to $x$. $\Phi^{-1}(0.90) \approx 1.28$. So the 90th percentile is $z = 1.28$ SDs above the mean: $x = 1000 + 1.28 \cdot 200 = 1256$.
steps: **Recognize the reverse direction.** Given a percentile (probability), find the corresponding $x$-value. Two steps: percentile → $z$ → $x$.
**Find $z$ from the table.** $\Phi(z) = 0.90$ means $z = \Phi^{-1}(0.90)$. The table gives $z \approx 1.28$.
**Sanity check direction.** The 90th percentile is ABOVE the mean (above 50th percentile), so $z > 0$ ✓.
**Pattern lesson — percentile inversion.** Every 'find the cutoff' problem uses the same recipe: read the table backwards to find $z$, then un-standardize back to the original scale.

questions.prompt: Which z-value is used as the critical value for a 95% two-sided confidence interval?
questions.explanation: For a two-sided $95\%$ interval, $2.5\%$ goes in each tail. $\Phi(1.96) = 0.975$, so $z = 1.96$ is the upper critical value (and $-1.96$ the lower). This is the most-cited number in introductory statistics — the basis of countless 95% confidence intervals.
steps: **Two-sided interval setup.** A $95\%$ interval covers the middle $95\%$ of the distribution, leaving $5\%$ in the tails, split evenly: $2.5\%$ each tail.
**Find the upper critical $z$.** We want $z$ such that $P(Z > z) = 0.025$, i.e. $\Phi(z) = 0.975$. From the table: $z = 1.96$.
**Other common critical values.** 1.645 (one-sided 95%), 1.28 (one-sided 90%), 2.576 (two-sided 99%). These appear constantly in hypothesis testing — worth memorizing.

questions.promptTex: \text{IQ scores: } X \sim N(100, 15). \\ P(X > 130) = ? \text{ Use } \Phi(2) = 0.9772. \text{ Round to 4 decimals.}
steps: **Interpret.** About $2.3\%$ of the population has IQ above $130$. Roughly 1 in 44 people. This is the basis of many 'gifted' cutoffs (IQ > 130).
**Reverse direction reminder.** If you wanted the IQ above which $1\%$ of people score: $\Phi^{-1}(0.99) \approx 2.33$, so IQ $\approx 100 + 2.33 \cdot 15 \approx 135$.

questions.prompt: Why are so many real-world quantities (heights, test scores, measurement errors) approximately normally distributed?
choices: Because the normal distribution is the simplest distribution mathematically
By the Central Limit Theorem: quantities that are SUMS of many small independent contributions tend toward normal, regardless of the contributions' shape
Because the experimenter chose the units to make it normal
hints: Heights = many small genetic + environmental factors summed.
Test scores = sum of points across many questions.
Whenever you have 'sum of many small independent contributions' the CLT predicts a bell curve.
questions.explanation: The Central Limit Theorem: when a quantity is the sum (or average) of many independent contributions, its distribution approaches a normal distribution, regardless of the individual contributions' shapes. Heights, test scores, blood pressures, and measurement errors are all such sums — so they're approximately normal. This explains why the bell curve appears so often in nature and society.
steps: **The CLT in one sentence.** Sum of many independent small contributions → normal distribution.
**Why heights are normal.** Height = sum of many genetic alleles + environmental factors (nutrition, hormones, sleep, ...). Each contribution is independent and bounded — CLT kicks in.
**Why test scores are normal.** Total score = sum of points across many questions. Each question outcome is independent — CLT.
**The Galton-board connection.** A marble's final bin = sum of $n$ Bernoulli(1/2) choices. The Galton board is a *physical demonstration* of the CLT.

**Limitations.** The CLT requires (a) finite variance per contribution, (b) independence (or weak dependence). When these fail, the result isn't normal — e.g., income (highly skewed, fat tail) violates the conditions and follows a different shape.

questions.promptTex: \text{For } X \sim N(50, 10), \text{ find the value } x \text{ such that } P(X > x) = 0.025. \\ \text{Use } \Phi(1.96) = 0.975.
questions.explanation: Find the cutoff above which only $2.5\%$ lies. $\Phi(z) = 0.975 \Rightarrow z = 1.96$. Convert back: $x = 50 + 1.96 \cdot 10 = 69.6$.
steps: **Recognize the type.** Reverse direction: given tail probability, find cutoff. Two steps: probability → $z$ → $x$.
**Translate the tail.** $P(X > x) = 0.025$ ⇒ $P(X \leq x) = 0.975$. Find $z$ with $\Phi(z) = 0.975$.

**Pattern lesson.** This is exactly how 95%-confidence-interval cutoffs are computed: $\mu \pm 1.96\sigma$ contains the middle $95\%$. The $1.96$ shows up everywhere in statistics — same number, same reason.
